does: This paper develops a standardized framework and guidance for frontier AI companies to conduct and report on risks associated with their internal use of advanced models before public deployment. The authors create a harmonized risk reporting standard that addresses requirements across three major regulatory frameworks—California's SB 53, New York's RAISE Act, and the EU's General-Purpose AI Code of Practice—all of which mandate that developers document safeguards and residual risks from internal model use. The guide is practical and implementable, designed to help evaluation teams and safety researchers at frontier labs systematically identify, assess, and communicate risks arising from internal testing phases, using the example of Anthropic's Mythos Preview model to illustrate the gap between internal and external deployment risk management.
connects: This work builds on emerging regulatory requirements and safety governance frameworks that have begun distinguishing internal use as a distinct risk category requiring explicit oversight. It extends prior work on AI safety evaluation, responsible disclosure, and pre-deployment testing by formalizing the documentation and risk communication process. The paper responds to a gap in existing external deployment frameworks—such as those addressing public API access or commercial product releases—which typically do not account for the specific risks and exposure timelines of internal development environments. It also aligns with broader movements toward transparency and accountability in frontier AI development, drawing on precedents in other regulated industries where internal testing documentation supports compliance and institutional oversight.
enables: This standardized reporting framework enables several downstream developments: it provides a template that frontier companies can operationalize to systematically manage internal use risks and build institutional practices around safety during development cycles. The harmonized standard reduces compliance fragmentation and establishes common language across jurisdictions, facilitating clearer communication between companies, regulators, and oversight bodies. By formalizing risk reporting for internal use, the framework creates a foundation for regulators and safety auditors to conduct meaningful oversight of the pre-deployment phase, potentially uncovering patterns and high-risk use cases that should trigger additional controls. It also opens pathways for improved incident reporting and near-miss documentation during development, which could inform future safety standards and best practices for internal model deployment.
